Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty intense but also chaotic. I'd block out around 4-5 hours most days, usually in the evenings after my other commitments. I'm a visual learner, so I'd spend a lot of time creating diagrams and color-coded notes from the lecture materials. The problem was that my attendance was terrible because I was dealing with a lot of anxiety, so I relied heavily on catching up through the LMS and my own study sessions at home.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on how I was feeling mentally that week. When my anxiety was manageable, I could stick to a more regular schedule, but during bad weeks, everything would pile up and I'd have to cram. Deadlines definitely dictated when I'd push myself hardest. I tried to be consistent, but life and my mental state made that really difficult to maintain.
Q3: Usually it was either checking for new announcements or when I needed to watch lecture videos I'd missed. Since my attendance was so low, the LMS became my lifeline to know what was happening in the course. Sometimes I'd log in just to ease my anxiety about falling behind, even if I didn't have the energy to actually study. The notification emails would also prompt me to check in.
Q4: Yeah, eventually logging in became almost automatic, especially in the mornings with my coffee. I set it as my browser homepage so I wouldn't forget. It took a few weeks, but once I realized how much I was missing from not attending class, the LMS became essential. The routine helped manage some of my anxiety because at least I felt connected to what was happening.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me since I missed so many classes. I'm a visual learner, so being able to pause, rewind, and take screenshots of diagrams was incredibly helpful. The discussion forums were also useful because I could see how other students understood concepts I was struggling with. I watched over 240 minutes of video content because that's genuinely how I learned best.
Q6: I mostly ignored the peer interaction features beyond the discussion posts. With only one peer interaction logged, you can see I wasn't great at that. My anxiety made reaching out to classmates feel overwhelming, so I avoided collaborative activities when I could. Some of the reading materials I'd skim rather than engage deeply with because videos worked better for my learning style.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate relevance to assignments or exams. Visual content like videos or infographics always grabbed my attention more than dense text. If something helped reduce my confusion and anxiety about the material, it was valuable to me. I also appreciated activities that I could complete at my own pace without time pressure.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford mentally. When my anxiety was high, I'd focus only on required work because that's all I could handle. But when I had better days, I'd explore optional materials because I genuinely wanted to understand things deeply. Required activities carried this weight of obligation that sometimes made them harder to start.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge amount of my studying happened offline. I'd download the lecture slides and videos, then work with them on my tablet where I could annotate and highlight. I also used external YouTube videos to supplement concepts I didn't fully grasp. My physical notebook is filled with hand-drawn diagrams and mind maps that represent hours of studying the LMS would never capture.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd spend hours reviewing my printed notes and self-made flashcards without touching the LMS at all. I'd also study with downloaded materials during my commute or when I needed to be away from screens for my mental health. Group study sessions with a friend from another section happened completely outside the system. My exam preparation was largely offline once I'd gathered the materials I needed.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a video or PDF once, and then that material would become part of my offline study rotation for weeks. My downloads folder was basically my personal course archive. This was actually a coping strategy for my anxiety—having materials saved locally meant I wouldn't panic if the internet went down or the LMS was slow.
Q12: The hardest times were when my anxiety peaked, usually around midterms and when multiple deadlines converged. There were weeks where just opening the LMS felt overwhelming because I knew how much I'd fallen behind on attendance. My stress levels were really high, and sometimes I'd just shut down and avoid everything for a day or two. The sadness would creep in and make even simple tasks feel monumental.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or overwhelmed, I'd log into the LMS but just stare at it without really processing anything. My extracurricular commitments sometimes competed with study time, and I'd have to make difficult choices. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those modules until the last minute. The fatigue from managing high anxiety meant my LMS usage was often superficial during rough patches.
Q14: Yes, more often than I'd like to admit. Sometimes I'd have a video playing but my mind would be racing with anxious thoughts about other things. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really absorbing what people were saying. There were times I'd click through materials just to mark them as 'viewed' for my own peace of mind, even though I wasn't truly learning in those moments.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew the deadline was approaching. Part of it was anxiety-driven avoidance—the bigger the task seemed, the more my brain wanted to run from it. Sometimes I'd tell myself I needed to understand the material better before starting, which was just an excuse. My assignment completion rate shows this pattern; I'd eventually do most things, but rarely with time to spare.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my external motivation system since my internal motivation was pretty low. I'd see a spike in my LMS activity in the 48 hours before something was due. The structure of deadlines helped combat my tendency to avoid, even if it meant working under intense pressure. Without deadlines, I probably would have engaged even less consistently.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Positive feedback would give me a temporary boost and make me want to engage more. But critical feedback, even when constructive, could trigger my anxiety and make me avoid that area of the course for a while. I needed time to process feedback emotionally before I could use it productively.
Q18: The course structure with its regular video releases actually helped me because it created predictable touchpoints. Having nine online courses meant I was used to digital learning formats. The modular structure let me tackle things in chunks, which was better for my anxiety than facing one massive block of content. However, the pacing sometimes felt too fast for weeks when I was struggling.
Q19: The video content being available asynchronously was a lifesaver for someone with my attendance issues. The discussion forums being optional rather than mandatory reduced my social anxiety around participation. What made things harder was when materials required synchronous attendance or real-time collaboration—those I often couldn't manage. Clear deadlines helped, but very short turnaround times increased my stress significantly.
Q20: I can relate to this somewhat. High activity might mean someone is searching for understanding, watching videos multiple times, reading discussions—genuinely trying to learn but struggling to produce work. Anxiety can make you engage with materials repeatedly without feeling confident enough to submit anything. Sometimes you're doing all the learning but the final step of completing assignments feels like a separate, harder task.
Q21: This definitely resonates with my experience. My exam score was decent despite low attendance because I was studying extensively offline. Someone might download everything, study from printed materials, and only appear in the LMS occasionally. The LMS activity is just one channel of engagement—it doesn't capture the hours spent with a notebook and highlighter away from the screen.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show login patterns, which resources were accessed, and time spent on the platform. They track completion of digital tasks and participation in online discussions. But they miss the quality of engagement, the emotional state during studying, and all offline learning activities. They can't capture whether someone truly understood something or just clicked through it while anxious.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely caring about understanding the material, not just completing requirements. It's when I'm curious enough to look up additional resources or think about concepts outside of study time. For me, true engagement is when I can push through my anxiety because the learning matters to me. It's a mental and emotional investment, not just time logged on a platform.
Q24: The LMS would never show how much my anxiety affected every interaction with the course, or the mental effort required just to log in on bad days. It wouldn't capture the mindfulness exercises I did before studying to manage my stress, or the tears of frustration when things felt too hard. My resilience—getting back up after difficult weeks and continuing despite everything—that's invisible in the data. The sadness I carried through this semester shaped everything but shows up nowhere in my activity logs.
